topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The music swelled, a vibrant wave of celebration washing over the ballroom. I was mid-conversation with Sarah, laughing about some ridiculous anecdote, when a familiar silhouette caught my eye across the room. It was him. Mark. He was standing near the refreshment table, looking impossibly polished in a dark suit. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt like it lodged in my throat. I stopped mid-sentence, my smile freezing on my face. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up. I could feel a strange, dizzying pressure behind my eyes, and I found myself unable to process the visual information. Sarah nudged me, noticing my sudden stillness, but I couldn't respond. I just stared, my mouth slightly agape, watching him move, and the world around me seemed to tilt on its axis.

[story 2]

I was trying to look casual, nursing a lukewarm glass of champagne, when I spotted him. Across the crowded dance floor, amidst the glittering lights and the throbbing bass, there he was. Mark. My mind went completely blank. The noise of the party—the chatter, the clinking glasses, the upbeat music—suddenly receded, replaced by a high-pitched ringing in my ears. I gripped my glass so tightly my knuckles turned white. I took a half-step backward, my feet feeling strangely disconnected from the floor. My gaze snagged on him, and for a full five seconds, nothing happened. I just stood there, my expression slack, a slow, involuntary widening of my eyes betraying the sudden jolt that had hit me.

[story 3]

The air in the reception hall felt thick, charged with excitement and expensive perfume. I was navigating the throng of guests, trying to locate a familiar face, when I bumped squarely into him. It was him. Mark. The collision was minor, but it sent a jolt straight through me. I stumbled back, my hand instinctively flying up to steady myself against a nearby pillar. My vision tunneled momentarily; the colors of the room seemed too bright, the sound too loud. I blinked rapidly, trying to force my focus back onto reality, but the image of him—standing there, looking entirely too happy—remained imprinted behind my eyelids. I mumbled an apology, my voice sounding unnaturally reedy, and felt an intense, sudden need to disappear into the nearest shadow.
